channel.title: NYT > Arts
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: My 5 Favorite Places for Art in London
item.description: Our critic Jason Farago shares what you shouldn’t miss in a city undergoing a palpable cultural renewal.
item.dc:creator: Jason Farago
item.media:credit: Clockwise from left: Hunterian Museum at the Royal College of Surgeons of England; Photo by Hufton and Crow; Sir John Soane's Museum; Photo by Gareth Gardner; Fine Art Images/Heritage Images via Getty Images

item.title: Billy Magnussen Has Mixed Feelings on Playing ‘Punchable’ Guys So Well
item.description: As the lead of a new Silicon Valley satire on AMC, “The Audacity,” he plays a particularly poignant variety: the tech bro.
item.dc:creator: Alexis Soloski
item.media:credit: Erik Carter for The New York Times
item.media:description: “It’s their insecurities, their fears, their soft spots,” Billy Magnussen said, describing what attracts him to playing difficult men. “No one’s a villain in their own life.”

item.title: ‘Death of a Salesman,’ With Nathan Lane and Laurie Metcalf, Is Perfect for Our Time
item.description: Arthur Miller’s classic tragedy returns to Broadway, starring Nathan Lane and Laurie Metcalf. Yet again, it is a triumph.
item.dc:creator: Helen Shaw
item.media:credit: Sara Krulwich/The New York Times
item.media:description: Laurie Metcalf and Nathan Lane in Joe Mantello’s revival of “Death of a Salesman” at the Winter Garden Theater in Manhattan.

item.title: The Sense of Touch at Billboard Scale
item.description: Ann Hamilton, known for conceptual art installations, embraces a new era with scanner photography at the Cleveland Museum — and finds a tactile tenderness.
item.dc:creator: Walker Mimms
item.media:credit: Daeja Fallas for The New York Times
item.media:description: “If you look at the structure of the brain, so much of the circuity is devoted to the hands,” Ann Hamilton said.

item.title: ‘Euphoria’ Is Back. Here’s What to Know.
item.description: The third season of the scandalous drama premieres on Sunday, more than four years after the end of Season 2. Fuzzy on the details? We can help.
item.dc:creator: Esther Zuckerman

item.title: Protoje, One of Reggae’s Premier Ambassadors, Doubles Down on His Roots
item.description: The musician’s latest album, “The Art of Acceptance,” features stars of the genre (Damian and Stephen Marley) and fresh updates on nostalgic sounds.
item.dc:creator: Patricia Meschino
item.media:credit: Elliott Verdier for The New York Times
item.media:description: “We started recording the album with the lyrics, ‘Rastafari teach I how to forgive, I’ve been learning how to live and let live,’” Protoje said.

item.title: Why Beauty and Ugliness Go Hand in Hand
item.description: A new exhibition explores Renaissance views on what is attractive and what is hideous, and how one can’t exist without the other.
item.dc:creator: Emily LaBarge
item.category: Bozar, Center for Fine Arts (Brussels, Belgium)
item.media:credit: Szépművészeti Múzeum/Museum of Fine Arts, Boedapest, 2026
item.media:description: “Ill-matched Couple (Young Man and Old Woman),” by Lucas Cranach (1520-22) is among the over 90 Renaissance works that show what the curators describe as the “dynamic tension” between beauty and ugliness.

item.title: In This Film About Amy Goodman, Independent Journalism Is the Real Star
item.description: “Steal This Story, Please!” chronicles the life and career of the “Democracy Now!” host as it argues against corporate ownership of the news media.
item.dc:creator: Alissa Wilkinson

item.title: 5 Children’s Movies to Stream Now
item.description: This month’s picks include the newest installment in the “Ghostbusters” franchise and the highest-grossing animated film of all time.
item.dc:creator: Dina Gachman
item.category: The House with a Clock in its Walls (Movie)

item.title: The Teens of ‘The Testaments’ Follow Up on ‘The Handmaid’s Tale’
item.description: Chase Infiniti and Lucy Halliday explore the fierce potential and peril of adolescent girls in Hulu’s follow-up to “The Handmaid’s Tale.”
item.dc:creator: Salamishah Tillet
item.media:credit: Chantal Anderson for The New York Times
item.media:description: “We both definitely felt a weight of responsibility stepping into this because ‘The Handmaid’s Tale’ was loved by so many people,” Halliday, right, said.

item.title: MoMA Survey Shows How Marcel Duchamp Changed the Art Game
item.description: Marcel Duchamp flipped the notion of art’s value on its head. We need foundation-shaking badly today, our critic says, and a sweeping survey at MoMA is an arresting reminder.
item.dc:creator: Holland Cotter and Jonah Rosenberg
item.media:credit: Association Marcel Duchamp/ADAGP, Paris/Artists Rights Society (ARS), New York; Photo by Jonah Rosenberg for The New York Times

item.title: ‘We Are Fierce Competitors’: Live Nation Case Reaches Closing Arguments
item.description: Thirty-four states accused the concert giant of suffocating competition and driving up ticket prices. The company denies being anything but big.
item.dc:creator: Ben Sisario
item.media:credit: Allison Dinner/EPA, via Shutterstock
item.media:description: The Live Nation trial has been closely watched in the music industry as well as in policy circles, where it is a test of the Trump administration’s stance on antitrust enforcement.

item.title: A Stroke and a Coma Couldn’t Stop Matt Pinfield, Rock’s Big Booster
item.description: Matt Pinfield, the D.J. and onetime MTV host, returned to the music world months after using up yet another of his “nine lives.”
item.dc:creator: Brian Raftery
item.media:credit: Jamie Lee Taete for The New York Times
item.media:description: Matt Pinfield has been talking about his favorite albums and artists on the air for more than five decades.

item.title: Review: A George Lewis Premiere at the New York Philharmonic
item.description: George Lewis’s new piece brought in the percussion and piano quartet Yarn/Wire, in an evening that also featured the soprano Golda Schultz.
item.dc:creator: Seth Colter Walls

item.title: Hilde Limondjian, Met Museum’s Longtime Concert Impresario, Dies at 89
item.description: She gave New York debuts to artists like Cecilia Bartoli and Peter Serkin, and introduced new music by Philip Glass and others.
item.dc:creator: Adam Nossiter
item.media:credit: Andrea Mohin/The New York Times
item.media:description: Hilde Limondjian in 2003. Her excellent taste was complemented by her innovative use of the vast resources of the Metropolitan Museum of Art.

item.title: The Enduring Body and Soul of Martha Graham at 100
item.description: Her dance company, the oldest in the United States, celebrates its centennial in a series of mixed bills. But why aren’t all the dances by Graham?
item.dc:creator: Gia Kourlas

item.title: Five Science Fiction Movies to Stream Now
item.description: This month’s picks will have you seeing double, including a film in which Vince Vaughn plays current and future versions of a character.
item.dc:creator: Elisabeth Vincentelli
item.media:credit: 20th Century Studios
item.media:description: From left, Eiza González as Alice, James Marsden as Mike, and Vince Vaughn as Present Nick and Future Nick in “Mike & Nick & Nick & Alice.”

item.title: 9 New Movies Our Critics Are Talking About This Week
item.description: Whether you’re a casual moviegoer or an avid buff, our reviewers think these films are worth knowing about.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times

item.title: How Authors and Readers Feel About the ‘Shy Girl’ Cancellation
item.description: Major publishing houses risk unwittingly putting out books generated with A.I. tools. Authors and readers are frustrated, nervous and grasping for solutions.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Alter

item.title: Is a Big Album Dropping? You Might Want to Watch the Road.
item.description: A study found that traffic fatalities increased in the United States by nearly 15 percent on the same days as the biggest album releases.
item.dc:creator: Adeel Hassan
item.media:credit: Pavel Herasimau/iStock/Getty Images
item.media:description: Researchers from Harvard Medical School studied driver behavior data and found more smartphone use, and a lot more distraction, on days when the likes of Taylor Swift, Bad Bunny and Kendrick Lamar dropped new music.

item.title: Jimmy Kimmel Salutes Melania’s White House Surprise
item.description: The first lady addressed her relationship with Jeffrey Epstein in a prepared statement that the talk show host called “better than her movie.”
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix

item.title: ‘The Pitt’ Season 2, Episode 14 Recap: Physician, Heal Thyself
item.description: Dr. Robby may be stressed out by the constant demands of the E.R., but the work may also be the only thing saving him from himself.
item.dc:creator: Sean T. Collins
item.media:credit: Warrick Page/HBO Max
item.media:description: Dr. Robby (Noah Wyle) took a moment to be alone with his thoughts. That might not be a good thing right now.

item.title: Afrika Bambaataa, Often Called the ‘Godfather of Hip-Hop,’ Is Dead
item.description: A pioneering rapper and D.J. from the Bronx, he was a key force in propelling hip-hop into the mainstream. He was accused of child sexual abuse later in his career.
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Abrams and Hannah Ziegler
item.media:credit: Scott Gries/Getty Images
item.media:description: Afrika Bambaataa in 2006. Born Lance Taylor, he was hailed as a master of records and drew from a wide range of genres and sources.

item.title: Pink Will Host the 2026 Tony Awards
item.description: Songs by the pop singer-songwriter are part of the Broadway shows “& Juliet” and “Moulin Rouge! The Musical.”
item.dc:creator: Michael Paulson
item.media:credit: Chris Pizzello/Invision, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Pink, who has won three Grammy Awards, will host the Tony Awards at Radio City Music Hall on June 7.

item.title: Getty Center in Los Angeles Is Closing for Year of Renovations
item.description: The art museum will close to the public in March 2027 to replace its aging tram system and modernize some galleries.
item.dc:creator: Matt Stevens
item.media:credit: Jenna Schoenefeld for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Getty Center, which opened on the Westside of Los Angeles in 1997, contains paintings by van Gogh, Rembrandt, Rubens, Monet and Degas.

item.title: Review: At Gibney Dance, Reaping the Maelstrom
item.description: Searching for new repertory, the company succeeds best with two veteran choreographers, Jawole Willa Jo Zollar and Lucinda Childs.
item.dc:creator: Brian Seibert

item.title: ‘Malcolm in the Middle’ Review: Nostalgia’s the Boss of TV Now
item.description: The dysfunctional family re-breaks the fourth wall (and other household objects) in a sweet but slight reunion.
item.dc:creator: James Poniewozik
item.category: Malcolm in the Middle: Life's Still Unfair (TV Program)

item.title: ‘Exit 8’ Review: Round and Round and Round He Goes …
item.description: Tokyo’s subway stations sparkle, and the rules are many and clearly posted. But for one man, just getting to work is hell.
item.dc:creator: Manohla Dargis

item.title: ‘You, Me & Tuscany’ Review: Love in the Italian Countryside
item.description: Halle Bailey and Regé-Jean Page star in this fumbling romantic comedy, complete with vineyard romping, barrel racing and a sexy serenade.
item.dc:creator: Beatrice Loayza

item.title: Melissa Chiu Exits Hirshhorn Museum to Lead Guggenheim
item.description: Melissa Chiu is stepping down as director of the Hirshhorn Museum in Washington to lead the Guggenheim Museum in New York.
item.dc:creator: Zachary Small

item.title: Her Museum Was Surviving in Russia. Then the Threats Became Too Much.
item.description: Nailya Allakhverdiyeva tried compromising with the authorities so she could continue showing contemporary art. But the intimidation didn’t end.
item.dc:creator: Ivan Nechepurenko
item.media:credit: Mustafah Abdulaziz for The New York Times
item.media:description: “You cannot abandon the ship during a storm because you are afraid,” Nailya Allakhverdiyeva said.

item.title: Cannes Film Festival Lineup Spotlights Almodóvar and Other Heavyweights
item.description: The 79th edition of the festival includes films by revered art house directors like Pedro Almodóvar and Pawel Pawlikowski but few Hollywood titles.
item.dc:creator: Alex Marshall
item.media:credit: Christophe Simon/AFP — Getty Images; Patricia De Melo Moreira/AFP — Getty Images; Vianney Le Caer/Invision, via AP
item.media:description: Movies by Pawel Pawlikowski, Pedro Almodóvar and Hirokazu Kore-eda are among the 21 that will compete for the Palme d’Or at this year’s Cannes Film Festival.

item.title: Rosamund Pike to Star in ‘Inter Alia’ on Broadway
item.description: The actress, a star of “Gone Girl” and “Saltburn,” will play a judge whose personal experience as the mother of a son tests her courtroom approach to justice.
item.dc:creator: Michael Paulson

item.title: 5 Classical Music Albums You Can Listen to Right Now
item.description: The latest recording from Pygmalion, Messiaen’s “Quartet for the End of Time” and works written for Anne-Sophie Mutter are among our selections.

item.title: ‘The Boys’ Got Bigger Than Ever. That Seemed Like the Time to End It.
item.description: “I really just wanted to go out on top,” said Eric Kripke, whose sex-and-violence-soaked superhero satire just began its fifth and final season.
item.dc:creator: Emmanuel Morgan

item.title: ‘The Travel Companion’ Review: Friendship, Insecurely Fastened
item.description: A struggling documentarian sublimates his hurt over his roommate’s new girlfriend into his fixation on losing airline perks.
item.dc:creator: Natalia Winkelman

item.title: ‘Outcome’ Review: Keanu Reeves as a Washed-Up Star Making Amends
item.description: Keanu Reeves plays an actor covering up a sordid past in this misguided film directed by Jonah Hill.
item.dc:creator: Brandon Yu

item.title: ‘Newborn’ Review: After Solitary Confinement, New Horrors Await
item.description: The writer-director Nate Parker focuses on the societal costs of imprisonment in this drama starring David Oyelowo as a Queens dad who goes upstate on a family trip.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Kennedy

item.title: ‘Hamlet’ Review: (In)action Hero
item.description: This version reimagines Shakespeare’s play for the screen with an appealing dynamism, set within a well-to-do South Asian family in London.
item.dc:creator: Nicolas Rapold

item.title: ‘Fiume o Morte!’ Review: Scenes From a Mini Dictatorship
item.description: In this documentary, Igor Bezinovic casts nonactors to restage major episodes from when an Italian poet turned strongman ruled a city in what became Croatia.
item.dc:creator: Ben Kenigsberg

item.title: ‘The Christophers’ Review: Fine Art, Frayed Artists
item.description: Ian McKellen and Michaela Coel are terrific in Steven Soderbergh’s sharp-eyed take on art and money.
item.dc:creator: Alissa Wilkinson

item.title: ‘Faces of Death’ Review: Is It Real? Does It Matter?
item.description: A meta-remake of the 1978 horror cult classic is more about how we watch than what we watch.
item.dc:creator: Alissa Wilkinson

item.title: True Crime Streaming: In ‘A Friend, a Murderer,’ the Killer Was Close
item.description: This docuseries offers a rarely heard perspective, as three friends discuss the complex experience of learning that the violent killer terrorizing their community was part of their tight-knit group.
item.dc:creator: Maya Salam
item.media:credit: Courtesy of Netflix
item.media:description: In the Danish docuseries “A Friend, a Murderer,” Nichlas discusses how the revelation that his best friend of 15 years was a rapist and a killer has changed his life.

item.title: ‘Bunnylovr’ Review: Run, Rabbit
item.description: A young woman struggles with the pitfalls of an extremely online life in this confident and meandering debut feature.
item.dc:creator: Jeannette Catsoulis

item.title: Best Alien Books by Octavia E. Butler, Ted Chiang and More
item.description: In these science fiction books, extraterrestrial beings are sympathetic, horrifying and everything in between.
item.dc:creator: James S.A. Corey
item.media:credit: The New York Times

item.title: Stephen Colbert Skewers Trump’s ‘Double-Sided’ Cease-Fire
item.description: “The Late Show” host asked if being double-sided wasn’t a prerequisite: “I believe there’s a word for a single-sided cease-fire and it’s ‘murder.’”
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: Late Show with Stephen Colbert (TV Program)

item.title: Popeye the Art Icon
item.description: Plus: a Milan hotel in a 19th-century mansion, summery, New England-inspired cushions and more recommendations from T Magazine.

item.title: The Good List: 6 Things to Add Joy to Your Day
item.description: Indispensable emoji, undesirable friends and show notes for your life.
item.dc:creator: Melissa Kirsch

item.title: Wayne Perkins, Guitarist to the Stars, Dies at 74
item.description: Mr. Perkins worked with Bob Marley, Joni Mitchell and many others, almost joined the Rolling Stones and turned down an offer from Lynyrd Skynyrd.
item.dc:creator: Richard Sandomir
item.media:credit: via Michael Ochs Archives/Getty Images
item.media:description: Wayne Perkins in 1972. A versatile guitarist, he could adapt to a wide range of styles, including the melancholy pop of Joni Mitchell’s “Car on a Hill” and Bob Marley’s swaying “Concrete Jungle.”

item.title: ‘Ketamine Queen’ Sentenced to 15 Years in Matthew Perry’s Overdose Death
item.description: The sentence for Jasveen Sangha, who prosecutors say was known to customers as the Ketamine Queen, is the stiffest yet for those charged in the “Friends” star’s death.
item.dc:creator: Emmanuel Morgan and Matt Stevens
item.media:credit: Jordan Strauss/Invision, via Associated Press
item.media:description: The medical examiner’s office attributed the actor Matthew Perry’s death to the “acute effects” of ketamine after he was found unresponsive in a hot tub at his Los Angeles home on Oct. 28, 2023.

item.title: Duke Ellington and John Adams: Titans of Classical Americana
item.description: Recent performances by the Orchestra of St. Luke’s and the Philadelphia Orchestra offered music by Duke Ellington and John Adams.
item.dc:creator: Seth Colter Walls

item.title: Matisse Is a Crowd-Pleaser. Here’s What the Crowds Rarely Get to See
item.description: The Acquavella Galleries in Manhattan offer more than 50 works, many from private collections. The show caps a surge of exhibitions on the great painter.
item.dc:creator: Carol Vogel and Guarionex Rodriguez
item.media:credit: Succession H. Matisse/Artists Rights Society (ARS), New York
item.media:description: From left: Eleanor Acquavella Dejoux, Nicholas Acquavella, William Acquavella, and Alexander Acquavella with bronze sculptures by Matisse from “The Back” series, at the Acquavella Galleries in Manhattan. Their major show of Matisse works opens Thursday.

item.title: An Audacious $724 Million Building Reinvents LACMA
item.description: Two decades in the making, the David Geffen Galleries will offer an unconventional approach to art history and cement the director Michael Govan’s legacy.
item.dc:creator: Robin Pogrebin and Damien Maloney
item.category: Los Angeles County Museum of Art
item.media:credit: Damien Maloney for The New York Times

item.title: This Audacious Film Festival Should Be Better Known
item.description: Year in and year out, New Directors/New Films showcases inspired work worth your attention. The latest edition is especially impressive.
item.dc:creator: Manohla Dargis

item.title: Modern Love: My Husband’s Breakdown Was My Breakthrough
item.description: Stefanie Gunning had to reach her lowest point to realize what she needed for herself.
item.dc:creator: Anna Martin, Reva Goldberg, Emily Lang, Davis Land, Amy Pearl, Sara Curtis, Elisa Gutierrez, Jen Poyant, Lynn Levy, Daniel Ramirez, Dan Powell, Elisheba Ittoop, Marion Lozano, Rowan Niemisto and Pat McCusker

item.title: ‘Euphoria’ Creator Sam Levinson Talks Tragedy, the Internet and the End of the Show
item.description: Ahead of the Season 3 premiere on Sunday, the show’s creator discussed his reputation, the loss of Angus Cloud and the soul-searching that followed.
item.dc:creator: Joe Coscarelli and Chantal Anderson
item.media:credit: Chantal Anderson for The New York Times

item.title: Frankie Muniz Refuses to Stay in His Lane
item.description: At 40, he is a father, a NASCAR driver and back as the star of a “Malcolm in the Middle” revival. “I have unfinished business,” he said.
item.dc:creator: Ashley Spencer
item.category: Malcolm in the Middle: Life's Still Unfair (TV Program)
National Assn of Stock Car Auto Racing

item.title: 5 Minutes That Will Make You Love Detroit Jazz
item.description: The city’s music is spiritual, searching and defiantly local, even when it spreads worldwide. Hear tracks from Yusef Lateef, Geri Allen, James Carter and other Detroit jazz greats.
item.dc:creator: Marcus J. Moore

item.title: ‘Faces of Death’ Set the Bar for Hardcore Horror. It’s Baaack.
item.description: A remake of the cult horror movie raises questions about the approach to viewing disturbing videos in a new era.
item.dc:creator: Marc Tracy

item.title: Late Night Puzzles Over Trump’s ‘Mixed Message’ on Iran
item.description: “Everyone, most notably the people of Iran, were wondering if their civilization was going to die tonight. Well, good news, it didn’t,” Jimmy Kimmel said after a cease-fire was announced.
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: Late Show with Stephen Colbert (TV Program)
